meta.name: Inferior STEMI with RV Involvement (57 M) — 12-Lead Interpretation and Fluid-First Resuscitation

patient.name: Robert Lin
patient.chiefComplaint: Substernal chest pressure with shoulder/back radiation, nausea, and a near-syncopal episode at his workbench about 30 minutes ago.

history.hpi: 57-year-old male electrician was at his workbench when he developed sudden onset of substernal pressure radiating to the right shoulder and through to the back, with nausea and one episode of near-syncope when he tried to walk to the break room. Coworkers helped him to a chair, called 911, and brought him a glass of water. Pain has been ongoing 30 minutes, described as 7/10, deep and pressure-like, no positional or pleuritic quality. Has had hypertension for several years (well-controlled on amlodipine), is overweight, and was a smoker until 2 years ago. No prior cardiac history.
history.lastOralIntake: Coffee and a bagel about 3 hours ago
history.events: Sudden substernal pressure with right shoulder/back radiation and near-syncope on standing at workplace ~30 minutes ago; coworkers called 911.

scene.dispatch: Dispatch: 57-year-old male, chest pain with near-syncope at place of work. Conscious. Coworkers on scene at industrial workshop.
scene.location: Electrical contractor's workshop. Patient seated in an office chair just outside the workbay. Adequate lighting; clear path for cot.
scene.safety: Scene safe. Workshop tools secured by foreman. No traffic hazards in path.
scene.bystanders: Foreman and two coworkers present. Confirm event timeline; deny any chest trauma or fall. Confirm no PDE5 inhibitor use known.
visualCues: Middle-aged male in work clothes, seated, slumped forward
Hand pressed against mid-chest with fingers spread (Levine sign)
Glass of water on a small side table; partially drunk
Workbench with electrical tools visible behind him

phases.name: Initial Contact
phases.description: EMS arrives to find a 57-year-old male seated in an office chair at his workplace, pale and diaphoretic, with substernal chest pressure radiating to the right shoulder and through the back. He is alert but lightheaded; SBP is borderline at 90, HR is bradycardic at 54 with regular rhythm — the classic 'inferior MI with RV involvement' physiology. The teaching point centers on 12-lead interpretation: ST elevation in II, III, aVF with III > II and reciprocal depression in I and aVL points to an RCA culprit, and right-sided V4R confirms RV involvement. The treatment pivot is 'fluid first, careful with nitrates.'

clinicalPresentation.breathing: RR 20, lungs CLEAR (no rales — supports RV vs. LV failure)
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Weak slow regular pulse; cool, pale, diaphoretic; cap refill 3 sec; JVD noted at 30°
otherFindings: Lungs CLEAR — important reassurance for fluid administration
No medication history of PDE5 inhibitor use
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: I felt fine, then I got really lightheaded and the room went dim. My chest hurts deep through to my back.

expectedActions.shortAction: Primary survey; recognize hypotensive bradycardic chest pain as possible inferior STEMI + RV
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS Inclusion + step 2 — chest pain of suspected cardiac origin; assess rhythm and immediately address symptomatic bradycardia.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Inclusion Criteria: Chest pain or discomfort in other areas of the body (e.g., arm, jaw, epigastrium) of suspected cardiac origin, shortness of breath, associated or unexplained sweating, nausea, vomiting, or dizziness. May also present with CHF, syncope, and/or shock. Assess the patient's cardiac rhythm and immediately address pulseless rhythms, symptomatic tachycardia, or symptomatic bradycardia.

expectedActions.shortAction: Cardiac monitor; defib pads on (high-risk inferior MI pattern)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS Patient Safety #3 — defib pads on high-risk patients.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: Consider placing defibrillator pads on high-risk patients.

expectedActions.shortAction: 12-lead EKG within 10 min; look at II, III, aVF; III > II suggests RCA
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS step 4 + Performance Measure — 12-lead within 10 minutes; identify STEMI pattern.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: The 12-lead EKG is the primary diagnostic tool that identifies a STEMI; it is imperative that EMS clinicians routinely acquire a 12-lead EKG within 10 minutes for all patients exhibiting signs and symptoms of ACS.

expectedActions.shortAction: Acquire right-sided V4R to assess for RV involvement
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS step 8 — right-sided leads identify presumed RV infarct; do not preclude NTG but require hemodynamic vigilance.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: The location of the infarct does not preclude the use of nitrates. Right-sided leads are of no additional value if an inferior STEMI has been diagnosed and such findings (presumed RV infarct) do not preclude the use of nitroglycerin: however, continually monitor the patient's hemodynamic status and be prepared to resuscitate if hypotension occurs.

expectedActions.shortAction: Aspirin 324 mg PO chewed (4 × 81 mg chewable, non-enteric)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS step 5 — chewable, non-enteric ASA 162–325 mg.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Administer aspirin; chewable, non-enteric-coated aspirin preferred (162–325 mg).

expectedActions.shortAction: Two large-bore IVs (18 ga); set up for 250–500 mL boluses
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS step 6 + Shock step 8 — IV access; preparation for fluid in hypotensive patient.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Establish IV access.

expectedActions.shortAction: Focused SAMPLE; screen for PDE5 inhibitor use BEFORE any nitrate
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS step 7 — screen for PDE5 use prior to nitrate, especially critical in hypotensive patient.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: The use of nitrates should be avoided in any patient who has used a phosphodiesterase inhibitor within the past 48 hours.

transitions.if: II/III/aVF + V4R STE confirmed; fluid bolus given
transitions.condition: 12-lead identifies inferior STEMI with III > II; V4R shows ST elevation > 1 mm; crew gives 250–500 mL NS bolus before considering NTG and calls STEMI alert.
Crew gives nitroglycerin to a hypotensive RV-infarct patient before fluid resuscitation; preload-dependent RV crashes — profound hypotension within 90 seconds.

phases.name: RV confirmed; fluid resuscitation
phases.description: 12-lead shows 2–3 mm ST elevation in II, III, aVF with lead III > lead II — RCA culprit pattern; reciprocal ST depression in I and aVL. Right-sided V4R shows 1.5 mm ST elevation, confirming RV involvement. Crew gives a 500 mL NS bolus while withholding NTG; SBP improves from 90 → 102 with the first bolus and lung sounds remain clear. Crew calls STEMI alert and rapid transport to PCI center.
phases.triggerCondition: Inferior STEMI with III > II and confirmed V4R ST elevation; fluid bolus initiated with appropriate withholding of nitroglycerin until BP improves.

clinicalPresentation.breathing: RR 18, lungs CLEAR after fluid (no rales)
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Improving radial pulse with fluid; less pale; cap refill 2 sec
otherFindings: 12-lead: ST elevation 2–3 mm II, III, aVF; III > II; reciprocal ST depression I, aVL
V4R: ST elevation 1.5 mm — confirms RV involvement
Lungs CLEAR after 500 mL — supports continued fluid
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: I feel a little better lying back. The pain is still there but the dizziness is better.

expectedActions.shortAction: Activate STEMI alert; transmit/notify PCI center; flag 'inferior with RV'
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS Goal #3 / step 4 — STEMI activation drives door-to-balloon time.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Activate hospital-based STEMI system of care. Advance notification should be provided to the receiving hospital for patients identified as having a STEMI. All EKGs should be made available to treating personnel at the receiving hospital, whether hand delivered as hard copy or transmitted from the field.

expectedActions.shortAction: NS 250–500 mL IV bolus; reassess lungs and BP each bolus
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Shock step 9 + ACS step 7 caution — predetermined boluses to MAP goal; be ready for hypotension with fluid bolus.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Adult: Physiologic target: MAP goal 65 mmHg. Fluid goal of up to 30 mL/kg of isotonic fluid by administering rapid, predetermined boluses (e.g., 500 mL) unless the MAP goal is achieved, or pulmonary edema develops.

expectedActions.shortAction: Withhold NTG until SBP > 100 sustained; opiate alternative if needed for pain
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS step 7 caution — care with NTG when BP marginal; weigh risk vs. benefit; opiate alternative.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Care should always be taken when giving nitroglycerin when the patient's blood pressure is marginal. If used in this setting, the clinician should weigh the risk and benefit of nitrate administration over the administration of an opiate analgesic and be ready to respond to hypotension with fluid bolus or pressor.

expectedActions.shortAction: Auscultate lungs each bolus; stop fluids if rales develop
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Shock Patient Safety — recognize cardiogenic shock; stop fluids if rales develop.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: Recognition of cardiogenic shock - If the patient condition deteriorates after fluid administration, rales or hepatomegaly develop, then consider cardiogenic shock and withholding further fluid administration.

expectedActions.shortAction: Watch for AV block / worsening bradycardia (RCA supplies AV node)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS Patient Safety #1 — observe for dysrhythmias; inferior MI commonly causes AV blocks via RCA-AV nodal artery.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Observe for signs of clinical deterioration: dysrhythmias, chest pain, shortness of breath, decreased level of consciousness/syncope, or other signs of shock/hypotension. Assess the patient's cardiac rhythm and immediately address pulseless rhythms, symptomatic tachycardia, or symptomatic bradycardia.

expectedActions.shortAction: Minimize scene time; rapid transport to PCI center
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS Educational Pearl — ischemic burden time drives morbidity/mortality.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Key Considerations: Acute coronary syndrome may present with atypical pain, vague or only generalized complaints. Ischemic burden time is a risk for morbidity and mortality, EMS can help decrease first medical contact to intervention time/reflow by efficient scripting/training of safely minimizing scene time.

transitions.condition: Fluid resuscitation has stabilized SBP > 100; STEMI alert called; en route to PCI center; cautious nitrate now reasonable.

phases.name: Transport to PCI center
phases.description: Patient en route to PCI-capable receiving facility with STEMI alert active. SBP improved to mid-110s with 2nd 250 mL bolus. Lungs remain clear. Patient reports pain improved with positioning and fluid. Crew may now consider cautious NTG since SBP > 100 and the protocol explicitly states RV involvement does not preclude nitrate use — though hemodynamic vigilance remains. Bedside handoff includes full 12-lead packet (including V4R), onset time, and treatment given.
phases.triggerCondition: STEMI alert called and transport to PCI-capable hospital initiated; fluid resuscitation has improved hemodynamics; ongoing monitoring for AV blocks.

clinicalPresentation.circulation: Stronger pulse; warmer skin; less diaphoretic
otherFindings: No new AV block; no new ectopy
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: I feel a lot better. I'm worried about my heart but I feel better.

expectedActions.shortAction: Consider NTG 0.4 mg SL now that SBP > 100 (cautious; reassess each dose)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS step 7/8 — RV involvement does not preclude NTG; once SBP > 100 cautious dosing is acceptable with vigilance.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Nitroglycerin 0.4 mg sublingual (SL), can repeat q (quaque, every) 3–5 minutes if SBP greater than 100 mmHg. The location of the infarct does not preclude the use of nitrates. Right-sided leads are of no additional value if an inferior STEMI has been diagnosed and such findings (presumed RV infarct) do not preclude the use of nitroglycerin: however, continually monitor the patient's hemodynamic status and be prepared to resuscitate if hypotension occurs.

expectedActions.shortAction: Early notify receiving hospital of any change; deliver full 12-lead packet at bedside
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS step 11 + Documentation — early notification of changes; all EKGs to receiving hospital.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Early notification to receiving facility of any changes in patient condition or serial EKGs. Key Documentation Elements: The time of symptom onset; The time of patient contact by EMS to the time of 12-lead EKG acquisition; The time aspirin (ASA) administered, or reason why not given; The time of STEMI notification.

expectedActions.shortAction: Repeat 12-lead with any clinical change
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS step 4 + Patient Safety #2 — serial 12-leads encouraged; especially with clinical changes.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Performance of serial EKGs is encouraged for symptomatic patients with EKGs initially non-diagnostic for STEMI. Patient Safety Considerations: Perform serial 12-lead EKGs (especially if clinical changes are noted).

phases.name: NTG given without fluid first → crash
phases.description: Crew gave nitroglycerin to a hypotensive RV-infarct patient before any fluid resuscitation. The preload-dependent RV cannot maintain stroke volume against further venodilation. Within 90 seconds BP crashes to 60/36 and the patient becomes confused. Crew must stop NTG, drop patient supine with legs elevated, give rapid IV fluid bolus, and prepare push-dose pressor.
phases.triggerCondition: Nitroglycerin administered before fluid resuscitation in a patient with inferior STEMI and presumed RV involvement, causing precipitous hypotension.

clinicalPresentation.circulation: Thready central pulse; cold mottled extremities; cap refill > 4 sec
otherFindings: BP crashed within 90 seconds of NTG to 60/36
Patient confused and slow to respond
JVD now collapsed despite RV failure (intravascularly empty)

expectedActions.shortAction: Stop NTG; supine with legs elevated; recognize NTG-induced crash in RV infarct
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS step 7 caution — be ready to respond with fluid bolus or pressor.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Care should always be taken when giving nitroglycerin when the patient's blood pressure is marginal. If used in this setting, the clinician should weigh the risk and benefit of nitrate administration over the administration of an opiate analgesic and be ready to respond to hypotension with fluid bolus or pressor.

expectedActions.shortAction: Rapid 500 mL NS bolus pressure-infused; reassess
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Shock step 9 — fluid resuscitation to MAP 65; rapid pressure-infused boluses.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Adult: Physiologic target: MAP goal 65 mmHg. Fluid goal of up to 30 mL/kg of isotonic fluid by administering rapid, predetermined boluses (e.g., 500 mL) unless the MAP goal is achieved, or pulmonary edema develops.

expectedActions.shortAction: If fluid-refractory: push-dose epi 10–20 mcg IV q3–5 min (10 mcg/mL prep)
expectedActions.rationale: MATC Shock vasopressor step — push-dose epi alternative for fluid-refractory shock.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Vasopressors (shock unresponsive to IV fluids) titrated to physiologic targets: Epinephrine, 0.05–0.3 mcg/kg/minute. Alternative to a drip, push dose epinephrine may be administered: Prepare 10 mcg/mL by diluting 1 mL of epinephrine 0.1 mg/mL (1:10,000) in 9 mL of normal saline; Administer 0.01 mg/kg (0.1 mL/kg) up to a maximum single dose of 10 mcg (1 mL) q 3–5 minutes titrated to maintain goal MAP.

expectedActions.shortAction: Withhold further nitrates; opiate alternative for ongoing pain
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS step 9 — opiates as alternative when NTG contraindicated.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: If the pain is unresponsive to nitrates, opiates are an acceptable alternative. Morphine should be used with caution in unstable angina (UA)/non-STEMI due to an association with increased mortality.

expectedActions.shortAction: Early notify receiving hospital of decompensation
expectedActions.rationale: MATC ACS step 11 — early notification of any changes in patient condition.
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Early notification to receiving facility of any changes in patient condition or serial EKGs.
